files.Vision/product-vision.md:
# Product Vision

> What we're building and why it matters.

## Vision Statement

Canonic is a local-first document editor for product managers — built on Git — that makes version control, peer review, and AI-assisted thinking feel native to how good PMs already work.

## Problem We're Solving

Product managers write documents — PRDs, strategies, roadmaps — in tools that treat them as ephemeral notes. Notion, Google Docs, and Confluence have no concept of history, branching, or structured review. Decisions get made in comments that disappear. `Final_v3_APPROVED.docx` is still the state of the art.

The real problem isn't the format. It's that there's no shared ritual for saying "this document is ready to build from." Engineers have pull requests. PMs have Slack threads.

## Why Now

Three things converged:

1. **Remote-first teams normalized async review.** The "walk over and ask" fallback is gone. Docs need to stand alone — and when they don't, the cost is a sprint built on the wrong requirements.
2. **AI can now act as a thinking partner.** The bottleneck isn't writing speed — it's thinking quality. Claude can challenge your reasoning before you've committed to it.
3. **Local-first is having a moment.** Developers are exhausted by SaaS tools that hold their data hostage. PMs are starting to feel the same way.

## Who This Is For

B2B product managers at 20–200 person companies who ship software. They write documents before building things. They collaborate with engineers and designers who have opinions. They care about their decisions aging well — they want to look back at *why* they decided something, not just *what*.

Not for: enterprise PMOs, agencies, or anyone who needs a PM tool to also be a project tracker.
files.Vision/north-star-metric.md:
# North Star Metric

> The one metric that best captures whether we're delivering value.

## Our North Star

**Committed documents per active workspace per month** — how many documents a user saves a checkpoint commit on.

Current baseline: 0 (pre-launch). Target at 90 days: 3+ committed docs per active workspace per month.

## Why This Metric

A commit is a meaningful act. It means the user trusted the document enough to snapshot it. It's the PM equivalent of merging a PR. If users aren't committing, they're using Canonic like a notes app — which means we haven't solved the alignment problem, we've just replaced Notion with a different notes app.

High commit frequency signals: the user understands the value of version control, they have something worth preserving, and they're building a habit around it.

## Leading Indicators

- Time-to-first-commit per new workspace (are they getting it?)
- Sessions per week per user (are they coming back?)
- AI messages per session (are they thinking harder before committing?)
- Comments added per document (are they collaborating?)
files.Strategy/strategy.md:
# Strategy

> How we'll achieve the vision given our constraints.

## Strategic Bets

**1. Win the solo PM first.** No collaboration features required to get value. The version history alone is better than anything a PM has today. Land the solo use case, then expand to teams. Don't build team features before solo is loved.

**2. Git vocabulary, PM framing.** Don't hide the model — explain it in context. "Branch" is a draft. "Commit" is a checkpoint. "Merge" is graduating a draft to main. PMs who understand the metaphor become evangelists. The README in every workspace explains this. Every modal explains this.

**3. AI as thinking partner, not ghostwriter.** Every other AI writing tool competes on output quality. We compete on decision quality. The AI asks questions, challenges assumptions, points out what's missing. It doesn't write your PRD — it makes your PRD worth building from. The system prompt is tuned specifically for this.

**4. Open source → paid hosting.** Release the desktop app free and open source. Sell the infrastructure later — hosted relay, sync server, team access management. This keeps the core trustworthy and gives us a credible enterprise path without pressure to monetize day one.

## What We're NOT Doing

- Building a project tracker. Canonic is for pre-build alignment, not sprint management. The moment we add assignees and due dates, we become Notion.
- Building a web app (v1). Local-first means local-first. The desktop app is the product. A web app would require us to store data, which breaks the trust model.
- Real-time collaborative editing. Async review is the right model for pre-build documents. Google Docs already does real-time better than we could.
- Replacing GitHub for engineers. This is PM-layer tooling. It sits above the code.

## Key Assumptions

- PMs will adopt a new tool if it clearly solves version history — the pain is high enough to justify switching.
- The Git mental model is learnable by non-engineers if the UI explains it clearly and in context.
- AI-assisted critical thinking is more valuable to a PM than AI-assisted drafting.
files.Strategy/competitive-analysis.md:
# Competitive Analysis

## Landscape Overview

The PM document space is dominated by general-purpose collaboration tools (Notion, Google Docs, Confluence) and task managers that bolt on docs (Linear, Jira). Nobody is building specifically for the problem of document versioning and pre-build alignment. That's the opening.

## Competitors

| Competitor | Strengths | Weaknesses | Our Differentiation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Notion** | Flexible, widely adopted, good for wikis | No real version history, comments get lost, no branching concept | Git-native history + named versions + branch drafts |
| **Google Docs** | Real-time collab, version history exists | Version history auto-saves every few seconds — useless for "what did we decide" | Meaningful commits you choose, not noise |
| **Confluence** | Enterprise adoption, structured pages | Terrible UX, no AI, nobody actually reads Confluence docs | Modern editor, AI that challenges, not just stores |
| **Linear** | Excellent engineering workflow | Documents are second-class citizens, no deep PM tooling | Documents as first-class artifacts with full git history |
| **Obsidian** | Local-first, powerful, markdown | No collaboration, no PM templates, no AI | Collaboration + PM-specific templates + AI thinking partner |

## Where We Win

Git under the hood means a real history model — branching, merging, named versions, diffs. No other PM tool has this because they built on databases, not files. That's not a feature gap we can close with a changelog modal — it's a fundamental architectural difference.

The AI angle is genuinely different: we tune the system prompt to ask questions, not generate answers. Most AI writing tools make you lazier. We're trying to make PMs think harder before they commit.

Local-first is a real differentiator for privacy-conscious teams. Your strategy never leaves your machine unless you choose to share it.
files.Planning/roadmap.md:
# Roadmap

> What we're building and roughly when. See [[product-vision]] for where this is headed and [[strategy]] for how we're getting there.

## Now (Q2 2026)

Active work — in flight or next up.

- [x] Git version control per document: commit, branch, merge, history
- [x] Named versions with restore (snapshot any commit as "v1.0", "approved", "sent-to-eng")
- [x] Fork document to a new branch draft
- [x] History panel with commit log and diff view
- [x] AI chat sidebar (thinking partner, not ghostwriter)
- [x] Inline comments anchored to selected text
- [x] Native system menu with workspace & settings actions
- [x] Persistent workspace sharing (auto-resume on start)
- [x] Excluded directories for sharing
- [x] Improved peer workspace browsing
- [x] AI "thinking" animation with pop-in response
- [x] Line numbers in peer viewer

## Next (Q3 2026)

Committed, not yet started. Building toward team collaboration.

- [ ] Agent web search — AI can pull in external research on demand during a conversation
- [ ] Agent inline suggestions — ghost diff lines the user can accept or reject without leaving the doc
- [ ] Terminal pane — run CLI commands in the context of the workspace
- [ ] **Claude Code integration** — "update the app to reflect this PRD" launches a Claude Code session pointed at the committed document

## Next Hires

1.  **Product Designer (Senior)**: Focus on complex state management in the UI.
2.  **Growth Engineer**: Viral loops for local sharing.
3.  **Customer Success (PM background)**: High-touch onboarding for teams.
files.Discovery/problem-statement.md:
# Problem Statement

> A crisp definition of the problem we're solving.

## The Problem

Product managers struggle to create a shared, authoritative version of a document that the team trusts enough to build from — because the tools they use treat documents as living notes rather than versioned artifacts. This means engineering teams often start building before the PM is done thinking, and "alignment" is achieved through Slack threads instead of reviewed, committed documents.

## Evidence

- `Final_v3_APPROVED.docx` is a real naming convention used at companies with $1B+ ARR
- Notion's version history records every keystroke — so it's technically there, but useless for reconstructing a decision
- Engineers regularly describe getting requirement changes mid-sprint via Slack DM
- In user interviews: "I can tell you what the doc says today but I can't tell you what changed last week or why we changed it"
- The workaround (manual versioning in filenames) is universal and universally hated
files.Discovery/user-research.md:
# User Research

> What we've learned directly from users.

## Key Findings

### Finding 1: Version history is the #1 unmet need

Every PM interviewed had a story about a decision they couldn't reconstruct. "I know we changed this requirement but I can't find where we landed or why."

### Finding 2: The "draft → review → approved" ritual is informal and fragile

Most teams have an implicit flow but no tooling to support it. Approval happens via a Slack message or a "LGTM" comment in Notion.

### Finding 3: PMs are open to Git concepts when explained in their language

"Branch" and "commit" land when you call them "draft" and "checkpoint."

Vision/product-vision.md.text: Ask Priya whether 200-person company is really the right upper bound — feels high for the solo PM story.

anchor.quotedText: B2B product managers at 20–200 person companies who ship software

Vision/product-vision.md.author: Priya Nair

anchor.quotedText: B2B product managers at 20–200 person companies who ship software

Vision/product-vision.md.text: The upper bound worries me a bit — 200-person companies often already have process. I'd tighten to 20–100 and add 'no dedicated program manager layer' as a qualifier. That's the person who's actually in pain.

replies.author: Ben Okafor
replies.text: Agree — 20–100 matches the design partners we talked to. Anything bigger already has a PMO.

replies.author: You
replies.text: Updated the vision to 20–100. Thanks both.

Planning/roadmap.md.author: Ben Okafor
Planning/roadmap.md.text: Huge for the team workflow. I hate having to remind people to turn on sharing every morning. Now I can just browse their docs as soon as they're online.

Planning/roadmap.md.author: Priya Nair
Planning/roadmap.md.text: I've added the 'Private/' folder to my exclusion list in Settings. It's nice to know my rough hiring notes won't accidentally leak while I'm sharing my roadmap.
